No empty fields nor ... are allowed. Do not skip any field.

When I first opened a social media account, I was curious about how others shared their lives, especially those who loved cinema or cycling like me. But soon I noticed that not everything posted was true. Fake content—stories made up, photos altered, headlines twisted—started to spread faster than the news of a football match. As a retired man with only a lower secondary education, I do not have the technical background to dissect every algorithm, yet my curiosity pushes me to learn how to protect myself and those around me.

The first step is to remember that social media platforms are designed for engagement, not accuracy. They reward sensational posts because they keep users scrolling. Therefore, a headline that sounds dramatic will often be shared before anyone checks its source. I remind myself to pause: if something feels too good or too bad to be true, it probably needs verification.

I use simple tools that do not require advanced skills. For images, I right‑click and search the image on Google; if the picture appears in a different context, I know it has been repurposed. For news articles, I look for the original publisher’s website rather than clicking the link directly from a social media post. Many reputable outlets have their own apps or websites that are less likely to be manipulated.

Another habit is to cross‑check with multiple sources. If a claim appears on one page but not on others, especially those known for fact‑checking like Reuters or BBC, I treat it skeptically. When my grandchildren ask me about something they saw online, I explain that the internet can be a mirror of our own biases; we must look beyond the surface.

I also set limits on how much time I spend scrolling. One hour a day is enough to stay connected with friends and family, but it gives me the chance to pause and think critically about what I see. When I encounter a post that seems dubious, I either report it or simply ignore it. Reporting helps platforms learn which content is harmful, while ignoring prevents the spread of misinformation.

Finally, I share my knowledge with others in my community. In local gatherings, I talk about how to spot fake news and encourage people to verify before sharing. Even though I am not a tech expert, my curiosity and willingness to learn have turned me into a small guardian against falsehoods in the digital world.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I have spent most of my life in Bari, watching the sea and the people who live by it. When I first opened a social media account, I was curious about how others shared their lives, especially those who loved cinema or cycling like me. But soon I noticed that not everything posted was true. Fake content—stories made up, photos altered, headlines twisted—started to spread faster than the news of a football match. As a retired man with only a lower secondary education, I do not have the technical background to dissect every algorithm, yet my curiosity pushes me to learn how to protect myself and those around me.

The first step is to remember that social media platforms are designed for engagement, not accuracy. They reward sensational posts because they keep users scrolling. Therefore, a headline that sounds dramatic will often be shared before anyone checks its source. I remind myself to pause: if something feels too good or too bad to be true, it probably needs verification.

I use simple tools that do not require advanced skills. For images, I right‑click and search the image on Google; if the picture appears in a different context, I know it has been repurposed. For news articles, I look for the original publisher’s website rather than clicking the link directly from a social media post. Many reputable outlets have their own apps or websites that are less likely to be manipulated.

Another habit is to cross‑check with multiple sources. If a claim appears on one page but not on others, especially those known for fact‑checking like Reuters or BBC, I treat it skeptically. When my grandchildren ask me about something they saw online, I explain that the internet can be a mirror of our own biases; we must look beyond the surface.

I also set limits on how much time I spend scrolling. One hour a day is enough to stay connected with friends and family, but it gives me the chance to pause and think critically about what I see. When I encounter a post that seems dubious, I either report it or simply ignore it. Reporting helps platforms learn which content is harmful, while ignoring prevents the spread of misinformation.

Finally, I share my knowledge with others in my community. In local gatherings, I talk about how to spot fake news and encourage people to verify before sharing. Even though I am not a tech expert, my curiosity and willingness to learn have turned me into a small guardian against falsehoods in the digital world.

By staying curious, checking sources, limiting exposure, and educating others, we can all reduce the impact of fake content on social media.

Topic: managing fake content. 3. Use simple verification methods (image search, source check). 4. Emphasize time limit and critical pause. 5. Encourage community education. 6.
